solution: Multiply by the conjugate: \( \sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x+([[b]])} - x = \frac{([[a]])x + ([[b]])}{\sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x+([[b]])} + x} \). Dividing numerator and denominator by \( x \) (using \( \sqrt{x^{2}} = x \) as \( x \to \infty \)) gives \( \frac{[[a]]}{1 + 1} = \frac{[[a]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 (the two \( x \) terms in the denominator).
This has the wrong sign.
This assumes the radical cancels \( x \) exactly, giving 0.
This uses the constant term \( b \) instead of the linear coefficient.
This mixes the linear and constant coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to -\infty} \left( \sqrt{x^{2} + ([[a]])x + ([[b]])} + x \right) \). (Note the \( + x \), not \( - x \) — the sign that keeps this finite as \( x \to -\infty \).)
solution: As \( x \to -\infty \), \( \sqrt{x^{2}} = |x| = -x \), so we multiply by the conjugate: \( \sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x+([[b]])} + x = \frac{([[a]])x + ([[b]])}{\sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x+([[b]])} - x} \). Dividing numerator and denominator by \( x \) (a negative quantity, so \( \sqrt{x^{2}}/x = -1 \)) gives \( \frac{[[a]]}{-1 - 1} = -\frac{[[a]]}{2} = [[answer]] \). This is the negative of the \( x \to \infty \) analogue — exactly the sign care this skill is about.
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign (uses the \( x \to \infty \) result instead of accounting for \( \sqrt{x^2}=-x \)).
This drops the factor of \( \tfrac12 \).
This assumes the radical cancels \( x \) exactly, giving 0.
This uses the constant term \( b \) instead of the linear coefficient.
This mixes the linear and constant coefficients with the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Multiply by the conjugate: \( \sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x} - \sqrt{x^{2}+([[b]])x} = \frac{(([[a]]) - ([[b]]))x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x} + \sqrt{x^{2}+([[b]])x}} \). Dividing numerator and denominator by \( x \) gives \( \frac{[[a]] - [[b]]}{1 + 1} = \frac{[[a]] - [[b]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 (the two \( x \) terms in the denominator).
This has the wrong sign as well as forgetting the factor of 2.
This divides by 4 instead of 2.
This assumes the two radicals cancel exactly.
This averages the coefficients instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Factor out \( x^{3} \) inside the cube root: \( \sqrt[3]{x^{3}+([[a]])x^{2}+([[d]])x} = x\sqrt[3]{1 + \tfrac{[[a]]}{x} + \tfrac{[[d]]}{x^2}} \). As \( x \to \infty \), the \( \tfrac{[[d]]}{x^2} \) term vanishes fastest and \( \sqrt[3]{1+\tfrac{[[a]]}{x}+\cdots} \approx 1 + \tfrac{[[a]]}{3x} \) (binomial approximation), so the expression \( \approx x\left(1+\tfrac{[[a]]}{3x}\right) - x = \tfrac{[[a]]}{3} = [[answer]] \); the lower-order term \( ([[d]])x \) does not affect the limit.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 3 (the cube-root analogue of the square-root's factor of 2).
This uses the square-root factor of 2 instead of the cube-root factor of 3.
This assumes the cube root cancels \( x \) exactly, giving 0.
This has the wrong sign.
This uses \( \tfrac{2}{3} \) instead of \( \tfrac{1}{3} \) as the coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x^{2} + ([[a]])x + ([[b]])}}{[[c]]x} \). (Careful with the sign of \( \sqrt{x^{2}} \) as \( x \to -\infty \).)
solution: As \( x \to -\infty \), \( \sqrt{x^{2}+([[a]])x+([[b]])} \sim \sqrt{x^{2}} = |x| = -x \) (since \( x < 0 \)). So the ratio behaves like \( \dfrac{-x}{[[c]]x} = -\dfrac{1}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \sqrt{x^2}=x \), the sign that only applies as \( x \to +\infty \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong overall sign.
This mistakes the limit for the denominator's coefficient.
This wrongly assumes the radical is negligible compared to the linear denominator.
This uses the linear coefficient \( a \) from inside the radical instead of the leading behavior.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
